risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used computer-generated randomization to assign new reviewers to either the intervention or control group." Comment: This explicitly states a computer-generated random allocation sequence, which is a truly random method and supports low risk of selection bias for random sequence generation.

Quote: "We used computer-generated randomization to assign new reviewers to either the intervention or control group." Comment: This states how the random sequence was generated, but it does not describe any concealment mechanism preventing recruiters/enrollers from foreseeing the next assignment; therefore allocation concealment is unclear by Cochrane criteria.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A senior editor emailed those in the intervention group and invited them to participate in a new mentoring program... Those in the control group received no initial study-specific contacts and no intervention beyond standard journal practices" and "The control group performed their review... The mentee group was treated similarly except that they were advised at the start of the study of the availability of a specific named mentor volunteer and encouraged to discuss papers individually with that reviewer either by phone or email." Comment: Participants (new reviewers) and the personnel delivering the intervention (senior editor/mentors) were not blinded to allocation; the intervention was explicitly communicated to the intervention group and absent in controls. Because the outcomes were reviewer quality ratings and related perceptions, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence review behavior and performance, so this domain is high risk.

Quote: "Our main outcome measures were the mean review quality rating score for each reviewer, and the slope in change (improvement or deterioration) in editor-assigned reviewer quality ratings from their first review until the end date of the study. These were calculated from review scores recorded contemporaneously within our editorial management software." Comment: The outcome assessor was the journal editor(s), who assigned review quality scores; these scores were captured contemporaneously in the editorial management software. The paper also states "All peer review at our journal is blinded," and the editor-assigned quality scores are a structured 5-point rating of review usefulness. Although the editors were not explicitly described as blinded to allocation, the primary outcome is based on routine editorial ratings and is not a patient-reported or highly subjective clinical judgment outcome in a way likely to be influenced by knowledge of group assignment; the measurement process was standardized and recorded in software. The secondary outcomes were self-reported survey responses, but the domain is judged on the most vulnerable outcome set here, which is the editor-rated review quality score, and there is no clear evidence that allocation knowledge could bias this measurement.

Quote: "Four subjects randomized to the mentorship group never made contact of any kind with their mentors, and thus did not receive the intended intervention. We therefore present below the per-protocol analysis excluding these four subjects; however we also performed a corresponding intention-to-treat analysis that yielded essentially identical results (data not shown)." Comment: There was exclusion of 4/24 participants in the mentored arm from the primary per-protocol analysis, creating differential post-randomization loss/exclusion (0/22 controls vs 4/24 mentored; about 17% in one arm). The paper relies on a per-protocol analysis that materially deviates from random assignment, and ITT results are not shown. Under the stated thresholds, this is high risk of attrition bias.
